title: The Sonnets LXIII - Against my love shall be as I am now
content:
آ آ آ آ Against my love shall be as I am now,
آ آ آ آ With Timeï؟½s injurious hand crushï؟½d and oï؟½erworn;
آ آ آ آ When hours have drainï؟½d his blood and fillï؟½d his brow
آ آ آ آ With lines and wrinkles; when his youthful morn
آ آ آ آ Hath travellï؟½d on to ageï؟½s steepy night;
آ آ آ آ And all those beauties whereof now heï؟½s king
آ آ آ آ Are vanishing, or vanished out of sight,
آ آ آ آ Stealing away the treasure of his spring;
آ آ آ آ For such a time do I now fortify
آ آ آ آ Against confounding ageï؟½s cruel knife,
آ آ آ آ That he shall never cut from memory
آ آ آ آ My sweet loveï؟½s beauty, though my loverï؟½s life:
آ آ آ آ His beauty shall in these black lines be seen,
آ آ آ آ And they shall live, and he in them still green.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Shakespeare represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Shakespeare's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
